title: How Domain Boundary Violations Slowly Destroy Your Codebase
subtitle: Have you ever opened a “service” file and seen it reach deep into three or four other domains? Ever had to add a workaround like `forwardRef` just to make basic dependency injection work? Maybe you’ve watched a change in one area cascade, unexpectedly breaking features nobody expected.This isn’t just a code smell. It’s the symptom of a deep architectural disease: Domain Boundary Violation.This article unpacks what domain boundaries should be, how they get violated, why this is deadly in growing backend codebases, and walks through a real-world case study— complete with service names, real business cycles, and a prescription you can actually act on.

content.html: Have you ever opened a “service” file and seen it reach deep into three or four other domains? Ever had to add a workaround like `forwardRef` just to make basic dependency injection work? Maybe you’ve watched a change in one area cascade, unexpectedly breaking features nobody expected. This isn’t just a code smell. It’s the symptom of a deep architectural disease: Domain Boundary Violation. This article unpacks what domain boundaries should be, how they get violated, why this is deadly in growing backend codebases, and walks through a real-world case study— complete with service names, real business cycles, and a prescription you can actually act on.

content.alt: How Domain Boundary Violations Slowly Destroy Your Codebase

content.html: A domain boundary violation happens when: - Code in domain A (say, `LeadService`) directly manipulates, queries, or triggers business logic in domain B (say, `ProjectService`) — and vice versa. - Cross-domain workflows (business processes that span leads, projects, versions, assignments) are implemented “inside” one or more peer services, instead of in a higher-level orchestration layer. - Services reach into each other’s “private” area and call methods, update state, or worse, keep references to each other. This is rarely a conscious choice. Most teams know they should have clean boundaries, but features come fast, deadlines are real, and “just call the other service” works — at first.

items: Mutual imports / circular dependencies between services or modules.
Use of frameworks’ circular dependency workaround features (like NestJS’s `forwardRef`).
Unit testing one service suddenly needing the real implementation of others.
Bugs or regressions in “unrelated” parts of the system after a change.
Module files with repeated provider declarations, as teams try to “fix” runtime boot errors caused by cycles.
Tight coupling: Changes in one domain can unexpectedly ripple into others — hidden cost for new features.
Hidden side effects: A “simple” business change is never simple; understanding code behavior means mentally simulating several domains.
Fragile dependency graph: Adding a new cross-domain dependency easily breaks boot order, leading to hacks or frantic `forwardRef` usage.
Testing pain: No way to cleanly mock or isolate a unit — tests are slow, brittle, or just skipped.
Ambiguous ownership: When workflows are scattered, no team (or file) “owns” the business process. In a word? Entropy.

content.text: A Real Example From Our System

content.html:
Let’s ground all the theory. Imagine you have the following business domains:
- Lead Management (`LeadService`)
- Project Management (`ProjectService`)
- Lead Assignment (`LeadAssignService`)
- Lead Versioning (`LeadVersionService`)
- Project Patch Management (`PatchService`)
In a healthy version, each of these knows only about its data and operations. In a codebase with domain boundary violations, that breaks down.

items: `LeadService` injects `ProjectService` (with `forwardRef`) and vice versa.
`LeadAssignService` injects `LeadService` (with `forwardRef`), while `LeadService` calls into `LeadAssignService`.
`LeadVersionService` injects `LeadService` (with `forwardRef`).
`PatchService` injects a wide array of project/lead services, often with `forwardRef`.
When updating a project, `ProjectService.updateByPro()` calls `leadService.updateLeadProject()` (so Project depends on Lead).
When creating a lead from a project, `LeadService.createProfessionalProjectFromLead()` calls `projectService.create()` (so Lead depends on Project).

Assigning work/materials (`LeadAssignService`) may:
 — Check lead status.
 — Load project data.
 — Rebase leads.
 — Create lead versions.
 — Add/cancel jobs.
Lead versioning (`LeadVersionService`) reads and writes lead/project state as part of mirroring and rebase logic.
`forwardRef()` is everywhere: Used to suppress `Cannot instantiate X before Y` errors in DI.
Duplicate provider declarations: Module provider arrays have the same service listed several times. Not a runtime bug, but a clear sign of desperate “boot fixes.
Orchestration logic is fragmented: Business processes span several services, but no one file owns the whole flow.

content.html: In frameworks like NestJS, `forwardRef` delays dependency resolution to make circular graphs “work.” It’s harmless in super-rare edge cases (event-driven, plugin, or observer pattern), but in almost all line-of-business backends, its presence is a signal: your boundaries are violated.

content.text: How to Fix It: The Senior Engineer’s Solution

content.html: Spoiler: You don’t fix this with DI tricks. You fix it with better architecture.

- Application-layer services (sometimes called “lifecycle” or “workflow” services) own all cross-domain coordination.
- Example: `LeadLifecycleService` orchestrates lead creation, assignment, project sync, versioning, etc.
 — It depends on: `LeadService`, `ProjectService`, `LeadAssignService`, `LeadVersionService`
 — But those services do NOT depend back on orchestration:** their duties are focused (CRUD, invariants, not business process flow).

- Refactor method logic so domain services no longer reach “sideways” into peers.
- Application services call domain services in sequence, not vice versa.

- No `forwardRef` in any module.
- No duplicate providers in arrays.
- Each service’s dependencies are clear, acyclic, and run “down the stack,” not across or back up.

items:
Each workflow is implemented in EXACTLY one service.
- Domain services can be safely unit tested in isolation.
- Adding new workflow features means editing orchestrators, not patching every service file.

content.html: To conclude, architectural entropy is not an inevitable byproduct of a growing codebase, but rather a symptom of neglected domain boundaries. While tools like forwardRef may offer a temporary reprieve from circular dependency errors, they often mask a "deep architectural disease" that leads to fragile dependency graphs and "death by a thousand cuts" through tight coupling and hidden side effects.

Reclaiming your architecture requires moving beyond dependency injection tricks and embracing a Senior Engineer’s solution: the introduction of an orchestration layer. By centralizing cross-domain coordination into dedicated lifecycle or workflow services, you ensure that business logic is no longer fragmented across peer services. This shift allows domain services to remain focused on their specific invariants and CRUD operations while dependencies flow “down the stack” rather than sideways.

Ultimately, the goal is to transform a tangled web of mutual imports into a clean, acyclic system where ownership is clear, testing is simplified, and new features can be added without triggering a cascade of unexpected regressions. By breaking these cycles and cleaning your provider lists, you move from a state of architectural decay to a robust, maintainable backend.
